text: An Avalous esophagectomy is one of the most complex operations in the specialty of upper GI surgery. In this video, I'm going to be demonstrating the thoracic part of an Avalous esophagectomy, demonstrating the use of the FlexDex instrument.

Essentially for me, FlexDex is a specific type of needle holder that allows the surgeon the three hundred and sixty degree functionality of the tip of the needle holder for the purposes of suturing in the chest where there is limitations because of the presence of the ribs there is limited range of movement.

The surgeon uses the FlexDex instrument to perform an anastomosis between the healthy esophagus, visible on the right side of the monitor, and the gastric conduit, positioned on the left side, within the thoracic cavity.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of the FlexDex instrument during the thoracic part of the esophagectomy, highlighting visible tremors in the instruments due to floating thoracic ports without pneumoperitoneum, causing the instruments to shake.

The surgeon uses a flexible tip needle holder to catch the tail end of the suture, demonstrating its effectiveness in allowing suturing in various directions. The flexible tip exhibits slight tremor and reduced strength compared to a rigid needle holder, which is visually noticeable during the procedure.

The third technical challenge was For me to consciously remind myself coming from the space of a rigid needle holder to that of using a flexible tip needle holder now is that we have to hold the needle always perpendicular to the jaws. Sometimes we are reflexively putting the needle at an angle, but with FlexTex that is not required because the instrument itself has the capacity to give the surgeon the free range of movement. And the last and the most important thing is to make sure that we consciously remember to keep the elbow of the instrument, which is the jaw, the black part, parallel to the tissues. I find this instrument to be really useful. There is a learning curve, but it was very helpful in suturing in the chest.
